card.title: Why We All Have Lawns
card.teaser: A uniform green rectangle in front of your house is a 400-year-old status symbol you probably inherited without knowing it.
card.coverImagePrompt: An aerial view of an American suburban neighborhood at dawn — identical rectangles of perfect green lawn in front of every house, long dewy shadows, painterly, slightly melancholic

lessons.title: A strange kind of garden
lessons.body: Most of what you plant in a garden bears fruit, flowers, or food. The lawn bears nothing. You mow it, water it, fertilize it — and in return it just stays the same shade of green. Why did an entire continent decide this was the default look of home?

visual.imagePrompt: Close-up of freshly cut suburban grass with mower wheel tracks, soft morning light

lessons.title: Born in the English manor
lessons.body: The manicured lawn was invented by 17th-century English and French aristocrats. Only rich landowners could afford to maintain huge expanses of grass — it meant your estate was so wealthy you didn't need that land for crops or grazing. Keeping grass short required armies of servants with scythes. A green lawn was, quite literally, wealth you could see from the road.

keyTerms.definition: A long curved blade used to cut grass by hand, before the lawnmower existed

visual.imagePrompt: A sweeping 1700s English estate lawn with distant servants using scythes, a manor house in the background, oil painting style

lessons.title: Two inventions made it universal
lessons.body: In 1830, Edwin Budding patented the first lawnmower in England — suddenly one person could maintain in an hour what took a team of scythers all day. Then in post-WWII America, Abraham Levitt built the Levittowns: identical suburban homes with identical front lawns written into the deed. Homeowners were required to mow weekly. The lawn went from aristocratic flex to middle-class mandate in two generations.

keyTerms.definition: Mass-produced postwar American suburbs that codified the front lawn as a civic duty

visual.imagePrompt: Vintage 1950s suburban scene, a man in white shirt pushing a chrome reel mower across a perfect lawn, children waving in the background

lessons.title: The biggest 'crop' in America
lessons.body: A 2005 NASA satellite study estimated that turfgrass covers around 40 million acres in the U.S. — three times more than irrigated corn. Americans pour roughly 3 trillion gallons of water and 200 million gallons of gasoline into their lawns every year. It is, by area, the country's single largest irrigated crop — and none of it is edible.
lessons.funFact: The average American spends about 70 hours per year on lawn care — roughly two full work weeks.

visual.imagePrompt: Stylized map of the continental USA with green patches scattered showing turfgrass coverage, infographic style

lessons.title: A habit, not a law of nature
lessons.body: The lawn isn't a natural landscape — it's a cultural inheritance, a default that got copied from neighbor to neighbor until it felt inevitable. Knowing where it came from is what makes it a choice again. Some homeowners are now replacing lawns with meadows, clover, or native gardens — and discovering the front yard was never required to be a carpet.

quiz.question: Who originally popularized the manicured lawn?
quiz.explanation: English and French nobles used huge lawns as a visible display of wealth — they could afford land that produced nothing.

quiz.question: What 1830 invention made lawns accessible to regular people?
quiz.explanation: Edwin Budding patented the first lawnmower in 1830, replacing teams of scythe-wielding servants.

quiz.question: Roughly how large is U.S. turfgrass coverage compared to irrigated corn?
quiz.explanation: NASA's 2005 estimate put turfgrass at about 40 million acres — roughly three times the area of irrigated corn.

sources.title: Mapping and Modeling the Biogeochemical Cycling of Turf Grasses in the United States
American Green: The Obsessive Quest for the Perfect Lawn
